Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd usually wait until I felt like I absolutely had to do something, then cram in a few hours here and there. With 14 online courses to juggle and limited resources, I couldn't really afford to be structured about it. I'd often study late at night when my anxiety would spike and I couldn't sleep anyway. Most of my actual learning happened in bursts rather than any consistent schedule.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was due and how overwhelmed I felt. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, other weeks I'd be up until 3am trying to catch up. Deadlines definitely drove when I studied more than any personal discipline. I have extracurriculars that eat into my time too, so I'd squeeze studying around those commitments. Regular study times just never really happened for me.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline looming or a notification that something was due soon. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important, which my anxiety made me do compulsively at times. If a peer from my support group mentioned something about the course, I'd jump on to see what they were talking about. It was rarely because I was excited to learn something new, more like damage control.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never felt like a natural part of my day. There were brief periods where I'd check in more regularly after connecting with some peers who kept me accountable, but that faded. I think with so many courses running simultaneously, no single platform ever became routine. It always felt like another thing I had to force myself to do.
Q5: The videos were genuinely helpful because I'm a visual learner and I could watch them on my own time. I probably watched over four hours worth because I could pause, rewind, and really absorb things visually. The peer interactions were also valuable when I actually engaged with them because hearing how others understood concepts helped me see things differently. Discussion posts helped too, though I didn't contribute as much as I probably should have.
Q6: I basically ignored most of the assignments until the last possible moment, which is why my completion rate is so low. The readings I'd often skip entirely or just skim because walls of text don't work for my brain. Anything that felt optional or wasn't immediately connected to my grade got pushed aside. With limited resources and time, I had to prioritize ruthlessly, and a lot fell through the cracks.
Q7: If I could see a direct connection to the exam or if it helped me understand something I was struggling with, it felt worth it. Visual content like diagrams, videos, or infographics grabbed my attention more than text-heavy materials. Also, when my peer support group discussed something, I'd feel more motivated to engage with it because there was a social element. Anything that felt like busywork got deprioritized immediately.
Q8: Required activities felt like obligations hanging over my head, adding to my anxiety. Optional ones I'd tell myself I'd get to eventually, but I rarely did because why add more to my plate? There was less guilt attached to skipping optional stuff, so they were easier to ignore. The required ones at least had deadlines that forced action, even if that action came at the last minute.
Q9: Absolutely. I'd download lecture slides and videos when I had good wifi access and then study them offline for hours. A lot of my actual studying happened with printed notes and diagrams I'd sketch out myself. I also used YouTube videos and free resources online to supplement what the course offered. My LMS activity definitely doesn't reflect how much time I spent with the material offline.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd be at my desk with printed materials and handwritten notes, working through concepts for hours. I'd often study with peers in person, discussing exam topics and quizzing each other, none of which showed up online. Sometimes I'd watch a video once on the LMS, then replay it mentally while sketching out diagrams on paper. My exam score reflects learning that happened largely outside the platform.
Q11: All the time. I'd access a video or PDF once, download it, and then that material would become my primary study resource offline. With my internet access being what it is and limited resources, downloading and working offline was more practical. I'd revisit those downloaded materials multiple times before exams. The LMS would only show that single initial access, not the hours I spent with it afterward.
Q12: My anxiety would sometimes get so bad that I'd freeze up completely and avoid the course for days. When multiple deadlines across different courses piled up, I'd shut down rather than tackle them systematically. There were periods where life stuff got overwhelming and school felt impossible to prioritize. My low resilience meant that once I fell behind, climbing back felt insurmountable.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or confused about what was expected, I'd just close the laptop entirely. The workload across 14 courses was genuinely unmanageable at times, so I had to make hard choices about what got attention. Fatigue made me less likely to engage meaningfully with anything. Competing responsibilities like my extracurriculars and limited financial resources meant school couldn't always come first.
Q14: Oh, constantly. I'd open readings or videos and just stare at them while my mind was elsewhere, worrying about other things. Sometimes I'd scroll through materials just to feel like I was doing something productive, but nothing was actually sinking in. My anxiety would have me logged in but mentally checked out. Those sessions probably looked like engagement but were really just me going through motions.
Q15: Assignments were my biggest avoidance area, which explains my 8% completion rate. I'd know they were important but the anxiety around starting them was paralyzing. I'd tell myself I'd do them tomorrow, then tomorrow would come with the same excuse. The bigger and more important something felt, the more I'd avoid it because the stakes felt too high to risk failing.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to act. I'd ignore the LMS for weeks, then frantically log in when something was due within hours. My response time averaging over 22 hours shows how I'd wait until the pressure was unbearable. Deadlines created panic, and panic created action, but it wasn't a healthy cycle. Without a deadline, I probably wouldn't have engaged at all.
Q17: When I got feedback, it could go either way. Positive feedback from peers or instructors would motivate me briefly to stay engaged. But critical feedback sometimes made my anxiety worse and I'd avoid that area of the course. The peer support intervention helped because getting encouragement from classmates felt less threatening than formal instructor feedback. Overall, feedback mattered but my response to it was unpredictable.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming with so much content spread across the platform. Having 14 courses simultaneously meant I couldn't give proper attention to any single course's structure. When modules were clearly organized with visual elements, I engaged more. But when things felt scattered or text-heavy, I'd disengage quickly. The structure needed to accommodate my visual learning style to really work for me.
Q19: Video content made engagement easier because it matched how I learn best. Clear deadlines helped because they forced action, even if last-minute. What made it harder was when there was too much reading or when instructions were confusing. The sheer volume of content across all my courses made it hard to engage deeply with any single course. Better visual organization would have helped me tremendously.
Q20: That could totally be anxiety-driven behavior, like compulsively checking the LMS without actually being able to commit to submitting work. Sometimes you're trying to engage but the actual submission feels too final and scary. Or maybe they're struggling with the material and keep reviewing without feeling ready to produce anything. It could also be perfectionism, where nothing feels good enough to submit.
Q21: That's literally my experience. My exam score was 86% and I got a Distinction, but my LMS activity was minimal. I did most of my real learning offline with downloaded materials and peer study sessions. The LMS captured maybe a third of my actual engagement with the course content. You can understand material deeply without constantly clicking around a platform.
Q22: Logs can show when and how often you accessed things, which reflects some level of engagement timing. But they completely miss offline studying, the quality of attention during access, and learning that happens through peer discussions outside the platform. They can't measure understanding or whether someone was actually focused. They show digital footprints, not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when you're curious and thinking about concepts even when you're not formally studying. For me, engagement happened most in conversations with peers and in my own head while reviewing downloaded materials. It's an internal state that doesn't always translate to visible online activity.
Q24: My anxiety and how it affected my engagement patterns wouldn't show up at all. The hours I spent studying offline with printed materials and peer groups are invisible. The emotional labor of managing 14 courses with limited resources doesn't register anywhere. And the fact that I actually learned the material well despite low platform activity, that disconnect between LMS data and actual learning is probably the biggest thing the data misses.
